title: Purchasing and Acquisition
description: Conroe ISD follows specific rules when spending public money to buy goods and services, ensuring purchases are made fairly and responsibly. These guidelines help prevent waste and require the district to seek competitive pricing on larger purchases, so taxpayer dollars go as far as possible. Community members can trust that school funds are being managed with accountability and transparency.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's Purchasing and Acquisition (CH Local) policy replaces the prior dollar-threshold trigger for Board approval with a broader "non-routine and discretionary" purchase framework requiring Superintendent notification, adds a detailed definition list of what qualifies as non-routine/discretionary, requires an annual vendor report to the Board, raises the competitive procurement threshold from $50,000 to $100,000, and adds a requirement for the District to adopt procedures to implement the policy.
changes_added: non-routine and discretionary purchases, not previously included in a Board approved contract or memorandum of understanding, that costs or aggregates to a cost of $50,000 or more to a single vendor over the course of a 12-month fiscal period beginning September 1 of each year shall require the Superintendent to notify the Board in writing approval before a transaction may take place. If two or more Board members timely raise a concern with the purchase to the Superintendent, the purchase shall be timely placed on a Board agenda for Board approval or disapproval. If Board notification is not possible without delaying needed goods or services for District operations, the District may purchase the goods or services but shall notify the Board as soon as practicable thereafter.
The Board shall be provided annually with a report of all District vendors paid in the previous fiscal year.
$5100,000 (competitive procurement threshold raised to $100,000)
$5100,000 (bids, quotes, and other proposals under threshold raised to $100,000)
The District shall adopt procedures to implement this Policy.
changes_removed: that costs or aggregates to a cost of $50,000 or more
over the course of a 12-month fiscal period beginning September 1 of each year
$50,000 (bids, quotes, and other proposals under threshold)
timeline_local.notes: The cover page does not contain a standard TASB action title heading (e.g., "Discuss," "Adopt," etc.) — it only shows the policy name and a rationale line, so action is marked 'unknown'. The redline uses red underline for additions and red strikethrough for removals; however, the competitive procurement threshold change appears as "$5100,000" in the document, which is a typographical redline rendering of the change from $50,000 to $100,000 (the "51" represents strikethrough "5" and new "1" overlapping). Added and removed items for the threshold have been interpreted accordingly. Much of the new "Purchasing Authority" paragraph text is red-underlined as new language replacing the old simpler dollar-threshold language.

compliance_assessment.analysis: This CH Local policy update contains one element that is Required by Law and five elements that are Locally Discretionary. The legally compelled element is the competitive procurement threshold increase from $50,000 to $100,000: SB 1173 (89th Legislature, Regular Session, 2025) amended TEC §44.031(a) to raise the aggregate threshold at which school districts must use a competitive procurement method from $50,000 to $100,000, effective September 1, 2025, and Conroe ISD's corresponding threshold change directly tracks that statutory amendment with no meaningful local tailoring. The remaining elements are entirely locally discretionary, as no verified Texas statute, TAC rule, or federal regulation mandates any of them. First, the 'non-routine and discretionary' purchase notification framework — requiring the Superintendent to notify the Board in writing before any such purchase at or above $50,000 to a single vendor over a 12-month fiscal period — is a local governance construct not required by TEC §44.031 or any other verified law; TEC §44.031 governs competitive procurement methods above the statutory threshold but imposes no board-notification trigger below that threshold. Second, the seven-item definition list enumerating what qualifies as 'non-routine or discretionary' (including non-recurring purchases, unusual purchases, pilot programs, district-level awards over $2,500, purchases materially differing from prior ones, purchases misaligned with specific local policies EMB/EFA/EFB, and purchases for professional services, consulting, technology systems, and large equipment) is a locally crafted taxonomy with no verified statutory basis; notably, TEC §44.031 expressly exempts professional services contracts from its own competitive procurement rules, yet the district locally chose to subject them to its new board-notification framework. Third, the two-board-member escalation mechanism — allowing any two board members to demand agenda placement and Board approval or disapproval of a challenged purchase — is a locally invented procedural check with no statutory mandate found in any verified source. Fourth, the annual vendor payment report to the Board is a locally chosen transparency measure; no verified provision of Texas law requires school districts to provide the Board with an annual report of all vendors paid in the prior fiscal year. Fifth, the requirement that the District adopt procedures to implement the policy is a locally imposed administrative directive; while consistent with good governance practice, no specific statute verified here compels this in the context of a CH Local purchasing policy. In sum, only the $50,000-to-$100,000 competitive procurement threshold change is compelled by statute (SB 1173, 89th Legislature, Regular Session, 2025, amending TEC §44.031(a)); all other added provisions reflect Conroe ISD's own local policy choices to enhance board oversight and transparency beyond what state law requires.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's Purchasing and Acquisition (CH) policy raises the threshold requiring Board approval and competitive procurement from $50,000 to $100,000, adds an exception for purchases made under Texas Education Code §44.031, and adds a new section on contracting with Historically Underutilized Businesses (HUBs).
changes_added: unless the purchase is made in accordance with Texas Education Code §44.031
$100,000 (replacing $50,000 as the threshold in Purchasing Authority)
$100,000 (replacing $50,000 as the threshold in Competitive Procurement Requirements)
$100,000 (replacing $50,000 in the 'Bids, Quotes, And Other Proposals Under $100,000' heading)

Contracting with a Historically Underutilized Business (HUB) — full new section: 'Nothing in Board Policy may be construed to limit or prohibit the District from contracting with historically underutilized businesses or businesses owned by members of a minority group or by women in accordance with applicable state law; however, all vendors, including historically underutilized businesses, must meet the same qualifications, performance standards, and requirements applicable to all District vendors.'
changes_removed: $50,000 (prior threshold in Purchasing Authority — replaced by $100,000)
$50,000 (prior threshold in Competitive Procurement Requirements — replaced by $100,000)
$50,000 (prior threshold in 'Bids, Quotes, And Other Proposals Under $50,000' heading — replaced by $100,000)
timeline_local.notes: The cover page does not contain a standard TASB action title heading (e.g., 'Discuss Revisions to...' or 'Adopt Revisions to...'); instead it shows only the policy title and metadata, so action is marked 'unknown'. The redline uses a layered number format (e.g., '$5̶1̶0̶0,000') where the red '1' is inserted and the '5' is struck through, indicating the threshold changes from $50,000 to $100,000. The new HUB section on page 3 is entirely red underlined, indicating it is fully new language. No text appears in red strikethrough for the HUB section.

compliance_assessment.analysis: This CH LOCAL policy update contains both legally required and locally discretionary elements. The threshold increase from $50,000 to $100,000 for competitive procurement and Board approval directly implements Texas S.B. 1173, 89th Legislature, Regular Session (2025), verified as enacted and effective September 1, 2025. S.B. 1173 explicitly amends TEC §44.031(a) to require that school district contracts valued at $100,000 or more (rather than $50,000 or more) in the aggregate per 12-month period be made by a competitive procurement method. The official Texas Legislature bill analysis for S.B. 1173 confirms this change, and multiple verified sources — including TEA guidance, the Texas Legislative Reference Library, and the Texas Department of Agriculture — confirm the September 1, 2025 effective date. Because TEC §44.031 sets the mandatory floor for competitive procurement, a district's LOCAL policy that previously mirrored the old $50,000 statutory threshold must now reflect $100,000 to remain legally compliant. The added clause referencing 'Texas Education Code §44.031' as a basis for exceptions is likewise legally grounded, tracking the statutory framework of §44.031(d) (board authority to adopt procurement rules) and §44.031(e) (subchapter supremacy with a HUB carve-out), and does not represent a locally invented provision. By contrast, the new 'Contracting with a Historically Underutilized Business (HUB)' section is a locally discretionary addition. Texas Government Code Chapter 2161 HUB obligations apply to state agencies, not independently to school district local board policies. TEC §44.031(b)(6) instructs districts to consider HUB impact when awarding contracts, and §44.031(e) preserves HUB laws from being superseded — but neither provision requires a school district to adopt a standalone HUB contracting section in its LOCAL board policy. S.B. 1173 updated HUB outreach obligations under Local Government Code §252.0215, but that provision governs municipalities, not school districts. The specific vendor-equality qualifier in the HUB section — 'all vendors, including historically underutilized businesses, must meet the same qualifications, performance standards, and requirements applicable to all District vendors' — is entirely locally drafted language not required by any verified statute. This section reflects a discretionary local policy choice, likely shaped by TASB model language, but not compelled by law. Accordingly, the threshold changes and §44.031 exception reference are Required by Law, while the HUB section is Locally Discretionary, making the overall change Mixed.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's Purchasing and Acquisition policy (CH Local) raises the board-approval threshold from $50,000 to $100,000, replaces prior pre-approval requirements with quarterly cumulative reporting to the Board, and adds a new "Vendor Alignment" section requiring vendors who interact with students to acknowledge and agree to district policies—explicitly prohibiting content on topics such as gender fluidity, Critical Race Theory, DEI, and anti-American sentiments.
changes_added: $100,000 (replacing $50,000 as the purchasing authority threshold)

shall be reported to the Board in a quarterly report. The District shall provide the Board a cumulative report in December, March, June and September, detailing year-to-date expenditures by vendor that exceed $100,000. Each report shall clearly identify all new vendors for the quarter and include the associated commodity category.
Vendor Alignment section heading (new bold/underlined heading)

To ensure alignment with the District's adopted policies, educational goals, community standards, and all applicable state and federal laws, any vendor or contractor providing services through direct interaction with students—whether in person or via digital means such as online courses, video conferences, video streaming, or other virtual platforms—or through the development, delivery, or distribution of print or digital materials, shall, prior to purchase, acknowledge and agree through a process designed by the Superintendent and approved by the Board to comply with the requirements of EMB (Local). This vendor alignment process specifically prohibits the inclusion of the following topics, except to the extent that the topic is expressly required as part of the Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS):
1. Gender fluidity, sexual orientation, gender, transitioning;
2. Pronoun usage inconsistent with biological sex;
3. Social and Emotional Learning Concepts that are divisive, conflict with District policy, and are inconsistent with the District's education goals;
4. Critical Race Theory;
5. Systemic Discrimination Ideologies;
6. Diversity, Equity, Inclusion (DEI); and
7. Unpatriotic, anti-American sentiments.

This requirement includes, but is not limited to services involving curriculum design, in-district professional development concerning direct student outcomes, athletic or professional coaching, student advisory services, specialized lessons, or similar educational or instructional support activities.

Failure to adhere to the terms of this Vendor Alignment, or any verified misalignment with the District's policies or community values as stated, may constitute grounds for immediate termination of the contract, subject to applicable contractual and legal provisions.
$100,000 (in competitive procurement threshold on page 3)
$100,000 (in 'Bids, Quotes, and Other Proposals Under $100,000' subheading on page 3)
changes_removed: beginning September 1 of each year shall require Board approval before a transaction may take place,
$50,000 (in competitive procurement threshold on page 3 — shown as $5̶0̶0,000)
$50,000 (in 'Bids, Quotes, and Other Proposals Under $50,000' subheading on page 3)
timeline_local.notes: The cover page title reads "Discussion of Possible Revisions to Board Policy CH (Local)", which maps to 'first_reading'. Dollar amounts in the redline use a strikethrough digit (e.g., "$5̶1̶00,000") to indicate the change from $50,000 to $100,000; context makes interpretation clear. The entire Vendor Alignment section is new (red underlined). All red-underlined and red-strikethrough text has been captured.

compliance_assessment.analysis: This policy update contains both legally compelled elements and substantially locally tailored provisions that go well beyond any statutory mandate. LEGALLY REQUIRED ELEMENT — Threshold changes ($50,000 → $100,000): Texas SB 1173, 89th Legislature (effective September 1, 2025), amended TEC §44.031 to raise the competitive procurement threshold for school districts from $50,000 to $100,000. All three threshold updates in the policy (board-approval threshold, competitive procurement threshold, and the subheading retitle) directly implement this statutory amendment. A district retaining $50,000 after September 1, 2025 would be in conflict with the amended statute, leaving the district no meaningful discretion on this element. PARTIALLY LAW-DRIVEN ELEMENT — Vendor content restrictions: Texas SB 12, 89th Legislature (signed June 20, 2025; effective September 1, 2025), extended DEI and sexual-orientation/gender-identity prohibitions to contractors and third parties providing instruction or programming to K-12 students (TEC §28.0043 as added by SB 12; see also TEC §28.0022(h) requiring discipline/termination policies for contractors who engage in prohibited acts). To that extent, some form of vendor compliance language responding to SB 12 has a statutory basis. LOCALLY DISCRETIONARY ELEMENTS — The following specific provisions are NOT required by any enacted Texas statute and reflect purely local choices: (1) The pre-purchase acknowledgment/agreement process designed by the Superintendent and approved by the Board — no Texas law requires a pre-purchase vendor screening or sign-off mechanism of this kind; (2) 'Critical Race Theory' as an enumerated prohibited vendor topic — this is not a defined statutory term in SB 12 or any other enacted Texas law applicable to K-12 vendors; (3) 'Systemic Discrimination Ideologies' as a prohibited topic — this category is locally coined and appears in no Texas statute; (4) 'Unpatriotic, anti-American sentiments' as a prohibited vendor topic — no Texas statute prohibits vendor content on this basis; (5) 'Social and Emotional Learning Concepts that are divisive' as a prohibited topic — no Texas statute enumerates this as a vendor prohibition; (6) 'Pronoun usage inconsistent with biological sex' as a vendor content prohibition — SB 12 restricts employee conduct toward individual students, not vendor content pre-screening at the procurement stage; (7) The quarterly cumulative reporting mechanism (December, March, June, September, year-to-date by vendor, with new-vendor identification and commodity category) replacing prior pre-transaction board approval — no Texas statute mandates this specific reporting cadence, format, or structure to the board; this is an entirely locally designed post-hoc accountability mechanism; (8) The contract termination clause for 'verified misalignment with the District's policies or community values' — a locally crafted enforcement remedy not required by statute; (9) The expanded scope of covered vendor activities (athletic coaching, professional coaching, student advisory services, specialized lessons, digital delivery via streaming/video conference) — locally defined beyond what SB 12's third-party instruction prohibition specifies; (10) The TEKS carve-out clause — locally tailored safe-harbor language not mandated by statute. Because the threshold changes are legally required by SB 1173 (89th Legislature) while the quarterly reporting mechanism and the bulk of the Vendor Alignment section represent substantial local tailoring and locally invented prohibitions that go well beyond what SB 12 or any other Texas statute requires of vendor contracts, the overall classification is Mixed.

description_local: This policy governs how the school district purchases goods and services, setting the rules for how money is spent on everything from supplies and equipment to contracts with outside vendors. It applies to district staff and administrators who make purchasing decisions on behalf of the schools. For parents and community members, this policy matters because it helps ensure taxpayer dollars are spent responsibly, fairly, and in accordance with state law.
